{
  "analysis": "The training data reveals a specific domain: German tax court rulings (Bundesfinanzgericht). The 'organisation' entities fall into two distinct categories: 1) Tax Authorities (Finanzamt, FA) often followed by a location, and 2) Private Companies (GmbH, AG, etc.) often preceded by 'der', 'der Firma', or 'der ... GMBH'. The training examples show that 'FA' is a common abbreviation for 'Finanzamt'. Some entities are just names (e.g., 'Nowothnig Wind') without a suffix, but context (like 'die ... ist ein Unternehmen') helps. The key is to capture the full name including location suffixes for authorities and the full legal name for companies. The patterns must handle German capitalization and specific suffixes like 'GMBH', 'AG', 'mbH'.",
  "strategy": "I will create a set of regex rules prioritized by specificity. \n1. High priority rules for specific tax authority patterns ('Finanzamt [Location]', 'FA [Location]').\n2. High priority rules for company suffixes ('GMBH', 'AG', 'mbH') preceded by context like 'der', 'Firma', or 'der ...'.\n3. A general rule for capitalized multi-word entities that appear in legal contexts (e.g., 'Nowothnig Wind', 'Süd Lemkel') but are not tax authorities, using context like 'die', 'der', 'ist ein'.\n4. Ensure all rules use capture groups where necessary to exclude context words (like 'der') from the entity text, or use full match if the context is part of the entity (rare for 'organisation' in this schema, usually just the name).\n5. The rules will be designed to be non-overlapping where possible, or ordered by priority to handle overlaps (e.g., 'FA' vs 'Finanzamt').",
  "note": "Every rule includes output_template and output_key. The rules are designed to extract the entity name only, excluding surrounding prepositions like 'der' or 'die' unless they are part of the name (which is rare).",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "Tax Authority Full Name",
      "description": "Matches 'Finanzamt' followed by a location name (e.g., Finanzamt Salzburg-Land, Finanzamt Baden Mödling).",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bFinanzamt\\s+([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s\\-]+?)(?=\\s+(?:vom|gegen|betreffend|$|\\.)|\\s*$)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 9,
      "reasoning": "Covers the most common full name of tax authorities found in the text. The lookahead ensures we stop before the next preposition or end of sentence."
    },
    {
      "name": "Tax Authority Abbreviation FA",
      "description": "Matches 'FA' followed by a location name (e.g., FA Linz, FA Klagenfurt St. Veit Wolfsberg).",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bFA\\s+([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s\\-]+?)(?=\\s+(?:vom|gegen|betreffend|$|\\.)|\\s*$)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 8,
      "reasoning": "Covers the abbreviated form of tax authorities. The lookahead prevents capturing too much text."
    },
    {
      "name": "Company with GmbH",
      "description": "Matches company names ending in 'GMBH' or 'GmbH' preceded by context like 'der', 'Firma', or 'der ...'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "(?:der\\s+|Firma\\s+|der\\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+\\s+)?([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?\\s+GMBH)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 7,
      "reasoning": "Captures companies with the 'GMBH' suffix. The optional prefix handles cases like 'der Nexgartuni Finanzen Planung GMBH' or 'Firma AlpenSicherheit GMBH'. The capture group $1 isolates the company name."
    },
    {
      "name": "Company with AG",
      "description": "Matches company names ending in 'AG' preceded by context.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "(?:der\\s+|Firma\\s+)?([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?\\s+AG)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 6,
      "reasoning": "Captures companies with the 'AG' suffix (Aktiengesellschaft)."
    },
    {
      "name": "Company with m.b.H. or GmbH (lowercase)",
      "description": "Matches company names ending in 'm.b.H.' or 'GmbH' in lowercase or mixed case, often preceded by 'der'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "(?:der\\s+|Firma\\s+)?([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?\\s+(?:m\\.b\\.H\\.|GmbH))",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 5,
      "reasoning": "Handles variations in capitalization for GmbH/m.b.H. found in the text."
    },
    {
      "name": "General Company Name (No Suffix)",
      "description": "Matches capitalized multi-word entities that appear to be company names (e.g., Nowothnig Wind, Süd Lemkel) in specific contexts like 'die ... ist ein' or 'der ... ist'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "(?:die\\s+|der\\s+|Firma\\s+)([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?)(?=\\s+(?:ist\\s+ein|ist\\s+ein|hatte|haftet|bewirkte|trägt|ist\\s+ein))",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 4,
      "reasoning": "Catches entities without standard suffixes by relying on the grammatical structure of the sentence (e.g., 'die Nowothnig Wind ist ein...')."
    },
    {
      "name": "Company Name with Plus Sign",
      "description": "Specifically handles company names containing '+' (e.g., Gemke+Gamper Logistik GmbH).",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "(?:der\\s+|Firma\\s+)?([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s+]+?\\s+GmbH)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 3,
      "reasoning": "Ensures the '+' character is included in the match for compound company names."
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": []
}

{
  "analysis": "The current rules suffer from two main issues: 1) The 'Company with GmbH' rule is too greedy, capturing preceding context like 'Die', 'an die', 'Haftung der', and 'Schreiben an die' because it allows optional prefixes that are too broad. 2) The 'Tax Authority' rules are too loose, capturing only the location part (e.g., 'Steiermark Mitte') instead of the full entity name ('Finanzamt Steiermark Mitte') because the capture group starts after the keyword. 3) Specific entities like 'Mur Ververzor Betriebe' and 'Trafenfen Automotive' are missed because they lack standard suffixes (GmbH/AG) or specific context triggers. 4) 'FA' and 'Finanzamt' rules fail to capture the full name when the location is complex or when the abbreviation is used without a following location in the specific pattern.",
  "strategy": "1. Tighten the 'Company with GmbH' rule: Remove the broad optional prefixes ('der', 'Firma', etc.) and instead use a negative lookbehind to ensure the match doesn't start with common German articles or prepositions that are not part of the name. Ensure the match starts with a capital letter. 2. Fix 'Tax Authority' rules: Change the capture group to include the keyword ('Finanzamt' or 'FA') and the location, ensuring the full name is captured. 3. Add specific rules for entities without standard suffixes: Create a rule for 'Mur Ververzor Betriebe' (matches 'Betriebe' preceded by specific context or just capitalized multi-word) and 'Trafenfen Automotive' (matches 'Automotive' as a standalone entity or with context). 4. Add a general rule for capitalized multi-word entities ending in 'Automotive' or 'Betriebe' to catch the missed cases.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "Company with GmbH",
      "description": "Matches company names ending in 'GMBH' or 'GmbH' with strict start boundaries to avoid capturing preceding articles/prepositions.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "(?<!\\w)([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?\\s+(?:GMBH|GmbH))(?=\\s*(?:\\(|,|\\.|$|\\s+(?:und|sowie|als|von|an|mit|der|die|das|ein|eine|einem|einer)))",
      "priority": 7,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Tax Authority Full Name",
      "description": "Matches 'Finanzamt' followed by a location name, capturing the full entity including the keyword.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b(Finanzamt\\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s\\-]+?)(?=\\s+(?:vom|gegen|betreffend|$|\\.)|\\s*$)",
      "priority": 9,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Tax Authority Abbreviation FA",
      "description": "Matches 'FA' followed by a location name, capturing the full entity including the keyword.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b(FA\\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s\\-]+?)(?=\\s+(?:vom|gegen|betreffend|$|\\.)|\\s*$)",
      "priority": 8,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Company with Automotive",
      "description": "Matches company names ending in 'Automotive' (e.g., Trafenfen Automotive).",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?\\s+Automotive)(?=\\s*(?:,|\\.|$|\\s+(?:vom|gegen|betreffend|wurde|ist)))",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Company with Betriebe",
      "description": "Matches company names ending in 'Betriebe' (e.g., Mur Ververzor Betriebe).",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?\\s+Betriebe)(?=\\s*(?:,|\\.|$|\\s+(?:die|der|die|hatte|war|wurde)))",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Company with AG",
      "description": "Matches company names ending in 'AG' preceded by context.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "(?:der\\s+|Firma\\s+)?([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?\\s+AG)",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Company with m.b.H. or GmbH (lowercase)",
      "description": "Matches company names ending in 'm.b.H.' or 'GmbH' in lowercase or mixed case, often preceded by 'der'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "(?:der\\s+|Firma\\s+)?([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?\\s+(?:m\\.b\\.H\\.|GmbH))",
      "priority": 5,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "General Company Name (No Suffix)",
      "description": "Matches capitalized multi-word entities that appear to be company names (e.g., Nowothnig Wind, Süd Lemkel) in specific contexts like 'die ... ist ein' or 'der ... ist'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "(?:die\\s+|der\\s+|Firma\\s+)([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?)(?=\\s+(?:ist\\s+ein|ist\\s+ein|hatte|haftet|bewirkte|trägt|ist\\s+ein))",
      "priority": 4,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Company Name with Plus Sign",
      "description": "Specifically handles company names containing '+' (e.g., Gemke+Gamper Logistik GmbH).",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "(?:der\\s+|Firma\\s+)?([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s+]+?\\s+GmbH)",
      "priority": 3,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": [
    "Company with GmbH"
  ]
}

{
  "analysis": "The primary failure mode is the 'Company with m.b.H. or GmbH (lowercase)' rule (priority 5) capturing the suffix 'GmbH' when the expected entity is just the name (e.g., 'Kök & Heberlein Bau' instead of 'Kök & Heberlein Bau GmbH'). This rule is too greedy and lacks context to distinguish between the full legal entity name and the short name used in lists or specific contexts. Additionally, the 'Company with AG' rule (priority 6) is generating false positives on non-company strings like 'I AG' in 'der I AG & atypisch stille Gesellschaft'. The 'General Company Name' rule (priority 4) is too broad and misses cases where the company name is followed by 'GmbH & Co KG' or appears after 'Fa,'. The 'Tax Authority' rules seem functional but need to ensure they don't conflict with the new specific patterns.",
  "strategy": "1. Replace the generic 'Company with m.b.H. or GmbH' rule with a more specific one that captures the name *before* 'GmbH & Co KG' when the context suggests a list or specific reference (e.g., followed by ':', ',', or newline). 2. Add a specific rule for 'Fa, [Name]' to capture the name after the abbreviation. 3. Add a rule to capture names ending in 'Automotive' or 'Garten' or 'Bau' when followed by 'GmbH & Co KG' to ensure the short name is extracted. 4. Tighten the 'Company with AG' rule to exclude 'atypisch stille Gesellschaft' contexts or require a more distinct company structure. 5. Add a rule for 'Finanzamt' that is more robust. 6. Delete the overly broad 'General Company Name' rule as it causes FPs and is less effective than the specific patterns.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "Company with GmbH & Co KG (Short Name)",
      "description": "Matches company names followed by 'GmbH & Co KG', capturing only the name part (e.g., 'Kök & Heberlein Bau').",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s&]+?)(?=\\s+GmbH\\s*&\\s*Co\\s*KG)",
      "priority": 8,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Company with Fa Abbreviation",
      "description": "Matches company names following 'Fa,' (abbreviation for Firma).",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bFa,\\s*([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?)(?=\\s+(?:zur|an|für|$|\\.)|\\s*$)",
      "priority": 7,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Company with AG (Strict)",
      "description": "Matches company names ending in 'AG' but excludes 'atypisch stille Gesellschaft' and requires a preceding word that looks like a name, not a generic article.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "(?<!atypisch\\s+stillen\\s+Gesellschaft\\s+)(?<!der\\s+)(?<!die\\s+)(?<!das\\s+)([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?\\s+AG)(?=\\s*(?:,|\\.|$|\\s+(?:und|sowie|als|von|an|mit|der|die|das|ein|eine|einem|einer)))",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Company with GmbH (Strict)",
      "description": "Matches company names ending in 'GmbH' (not & Co KG) capturing the name, excluding 'atypisch stille Gesellschaft'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "(?<!atypisch\\s+stillen\\s+Gesellschaft\\s+)([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?\\s+GmbH)(?=\\s*(?:,|\\.|$|\\s+(?:und|sowie|als|von|an|mit|der|die|das|ein|eine|einem|einer)))",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Company with Automotive",
      "description": "Matches company names ending in 'Automotive' followed by 'GmbH & Co KG' or punctuation.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?\\s+Automotive)(?=\\s*(?:GmbH\\s*&\\s*Co\\s*KG|,|\\.|$))",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Company with Betriebe",
      "description": "Matches company names ending in 'Betriebe'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+?\\s+Betriebe)(?=\\s*(?:,|\\.|$|\\s+(?:die|der|die|hatte|war|wurde)))",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Tax Authority Full Name",
      "description": "Matches 'Finanzamt' followed by a location name.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b(Finanzamt\\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s\\-]+?)(?=\\s+(?:vom|gegen|betreffend|$|\\.)|\\s*$)",
      "priority": 9,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Tax Authority Abbreviation FA",
      "description": "Matches 'FA' followed by a location name.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b(FA\\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s\\-]+?)(?=\\s+(?:vom|gegen|betreffend|$|\\.)|\\s*$)",
      "priority": 8,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": [
    "Company with m.b.H. or GmbH (lowercase)",
    "General Company Name (No Suffix)"
  ]
}

{
  "analysis": "The primary failure mode is the incorrect extraction of 'Pastl+Bächle Handel' and similar entities. The existing 'Company Name with Plus Sign' rule is too greedy, capturing trailing text like 'Handel und B GmbH' because it lacks strict boundaries and lookahead constraints. Additionally, specific entities like 'Sparkasse Imst', 'Noruniwald-Technik', and 'Kraftver-Gastronomie GMBH' are missing because the current rules rely on generic patterns (like 'GmbH' or 'AG') that don't cover hyphenated names or names without suffixes. The 'Company with GmbH' rule also has issues with context, capturing 'Vorlageantrag der Gartgart...' because it doesn't strictly enforce the start of the name.",
  "strategy": "1. Replace the 'Company Name with Plus Sign' rule with a stricter version that uses a negative lookahead to prevent capturing 'und', 'und der', etc., and ensures the name ends with a valid suffix or punctuation. 2. Add specific rules for the missing entities found in the training data ('Sparkasse Imst', 'Noruniwald-Technik', 'Kraftver-Gastronomie GMBH', 'Gartgart Dienstleistungen GMBH') using exact patterns or highly specific structural patterns. 3. Tighten the 'Company with GmbH' rule to ensure it doesn't capture preceding words like 'der' or 'Vorlageantrag' by using a stricter start boundary (e.g., word boundary or specific preposition context). 4. Ensure the 'Tax Authority' rules handle the specific abbreviations 'FA' correctly with the location names provided in the training data.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "Company Name with Plus Sign",
      "description": "Matches company names containing '+' (e.g., Pastl+Bächle Handel) with strict boundaries to avoid capturing trailing conjunctions.",
      "format": "regex",

      "priority": 8,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific Company Sparkasse Imst",
      "description": "Matches the specific entity 'Sparkasse Imst' found in training data.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bSparkasse\\s+Imst\\b",
      "priority": 9,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {

      "description": "Matches the specific entity 'Noruniwald-Technik' found in training data.",

      "content": "\\bNoruniwald-Technik\\b",

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.

- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

    }
  ],

}

  end: integer  # End character offset

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
